findings.summary: Case handler classes follow inconsistent protocols across sibling modules: NominativeCase and AccusativeCase lack PRECISION and VALID_TRANSITIONS class constants present in AblativeCase, LocativeCase, GenitiveCase, InstrumentalCase, and VocativeCase.
evidence: AblativeCase (line 24): `PRECISION = 1.1`, LocativeCase (line 24): `PRECISION = 0.9`, GenitiveCase (line 24): `PRECISION = 1.0`, InstrumentalCase (line 24): `PRECISION = 0.8`, VocativeCase (line 24): `PRECISION = 2.0` ā€” but NominativeCase and AccusativeCase have no PRECISION constant.

AblativeCase (line 27): `VALID_TRANSITIONS = [Case.NOMINATIVE]`, LocativeCase (line 27): `VALID_TRANSITIONS = [Case.ABLATIVE]` ā€” neither NominativeCase, AccusativeCase, InstrumentalCase, GenitiveCase, VocativeCase, nor DativeCase define this attribute.

AccusativeCase exposes two extra static methods not present on any other case class: `accept_updates` (line 117) and NominativeCase has no analogue. InstrumentalCase uniquely adds `execute_operation` (line 130) and `register_operation` (line 154). DativeCase lacks `get_parameters`, which is present on six of eight sibling classes.
findings.suggestion: Define a shared abstract base class or Protocol for all case handlers, declaring required class-level constants (PRECISION, VALID_TRANSITIONS) and a standard method surface (apply, process_update, calculate_free_energy, get_parameters). Each case module must then satisfy the protocol, eliminating the per-class drift.

findings.summary: CaseManager.case_handlers only registers NominativeCase and AccusativeCase, silently falling back to bare model.case assignment for the other six cases, contradicting the pattern established by the full eight-case class hierarchy.
evidence: Lines 28ā€“32 of case_manager.py: `self.case_handlers = { Case.NOMINATIVE: NominativeCase, Case.ACCUSATIVE: AccusativeCase, # Add other cases as they're implemented }` ā€” the comment acknowledges the gap, but the remaining six case handler classes exist and are exported from `__init__.py`.
Lines 77ā€“83: `transform_case` falls through to `model.case = target_case` for all non-registered cases, bypassing the handler classes entirely.

Lines 139ā€“146: `process_update` similarly falls back to `model.update(data)` for unregistered cases, meaning DativeCase, GenitiveCase, InstrumentalCase, AblativeCase, LocativeCase, and VocativeCase handlers are dead code from the manager's perspective.
findings.suggestion: Populate case_handlers with all eight case classes at construction time. If a case class is not yet production-ready, mark it explicitly with a feature flag rather than leaving the registry empty and silently bypassing the handler layer.

findings.summary: InsectModel introduces a redundant `self.current_case` attribute (line 92 of base.py) alongside the inherited `Model._case` / `Model.case` property, creating two parallel case-tracking fields that can diverge silently.
evidence: InsectModel.__init__ line 92: `self.current_case = initial_case` ā€” a plain instance attribute set directly, not via `Model.case` setter.
InsectModel internally reads `self.current_case` throughout (lines 337, 343, 353-354, 388, 584, 701), while `Model.case` property is backed by `self._case`. The two can differ if only one is updated.
Model.case setter (model.py lines 60-73) maintains `_case_history` and calls `_apply_case_transformation`; bypassing it with `self.current_case` skips both side-effects.
findings.suggestion: Remove `self.current_case` from InsectModel and route all reads/writes through the inherited `self.case` property. If additional case-transition logic is needed, override `_apply_case_transformation` rather than maintaining a parallel field.

findings.summary: src/core/model.py calls logging.basicConfig at module import time, which is a global side-effect that pollutes the root logger for any consumer of the library.
evidence: model.py line 8: `logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO, format='%(asctime)s - %(name)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')` executes unconditionally on import.

active_inference.py (line 8) correctly uses `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` without calling basicConfig. The root module establishes a contradictory pattern that every downstream importer inherits.
findings.suggestion: Remove the `logging.basicConfig` call from model.py. Add `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` and let the application entry point configure the root handler. All 57 importers of model.py currently receive the misconfigured root handler on first import.

findings.summary: Case modules mix bare `logging.warning/logging.info` calls with the module-level `logger` pattern used elsewhere in the project, creating two different logging namespaces within the same package.
evidence: nominative.py line 40: `logging.info(...)`, line 107: `logging.warning(...)` ā€” uses the root logger.
accusative.py line 39: `logging.info(...)`, line 76: `logging.info(...)`, line 111: `logging.warning(...)` ā€” same pattern.
insect/base.py line 17: `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)`, line 118: `logger.info(...)` ā€” uses named module logger.
active_inference.py line 8: `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` ā€” uses named module logger. No case module defines a named logger despite emitting many log messages.
findings.suggestion: Add `logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)` at module level in each case module and replace all bare `logging.X` calls with `logger.X`. This gives per-module filtering granularity and is consistent with the rest of the codebase.

findings.summary: All eight case handler calculate_free_energy methods catch bare Exception, log a warning, and return a hardcoded default float ā€” silently swallowing all errors including programming mistakes, with no way for callers to distinguish a computed result from a fallback.
evidence: nominative.py lines 96-108: `try: ... except Exception as e: logging.warning(...); return default_fe` ā€” default_fe=1.0.
accusative.py lines 99-112: identical pattern ā€” default_fe=0.5.
ablative.py lines 95-106: identical pattern ā€” default_fe=1.0 but uses `AblativeCase.PRECISION` multiplier before the exception, so the default does not reflect the same scale.

All eight case files follow this identical catch-and-return-default pattern, meaning AttributeError on a missing attribute, TypeError from a bad numpy operation, and MemoryError from a large array all produce the same silent warning log and identical numeric return.

CaseManager.calculate_free_energy (case_manager.py lines 185-206) has no exception handling at all, so if free_energy() raises NotImplementedError from the base Model, it propagates uncaught ā€” inconsistent with the swallowing pattern in every case class.
findings.suggestion: Narrow the catch to expected numeric/attribute errors (e.g., `except (AttributeError, ValueError, np.linalg.LinAlgError)`). Return a sentinel such as `float('nan')` or a typed Result so callers can detect the fallback. For `CaseManager.calculate_free_energy`, add a catch for `NotImplementedError` to match the handler behaviour, or document the inconsistency explicitly.

findings.summary: The core subsystem raises generic built-in exceptions (NotImplementedError, ValueError, TypeError, RuntimeError) while the lexicon subsystem maintains a full typed exception hierarchy (LexiconError ā†’ ConfigurationError, APIError, ProcessingError, etc.), with no bridging at subsystem boundaries.
evidence: model.py lines 123, 135: raises bare `NotImplementedError` with no custom exception type.
linear_regression.py lines 69, 80, 160: raises bare `ValueError` strings.
src/llm/OpenRouter/openrouter.py lines 212, 252: raises bare `Exception("Circuit breaker is OPEN...")` ā€” the most degenerate possible error type.

lexicon/core/exceptions.py: defines 12 typed exception classes (LexiconError, ConfigurationError, APIError, ModelError, InputError, GraphError, StorageError, ProcessingError, RateLimitError, AuthenticationError, TimeoutError, ParsingError).
lexicon/core/engine.py line 154: raises `ValueError` inside the lexicon subsystem rather than `ConfigurationError`, breaching its own hierarchy.
src/llm/ollama/client.py lines 158-165: raises `OllamaConnectionError` and `OllamaError` (a third, separate error hierarchy not visible in lexicon/core/exceptions.py).
findings.suggestion: Define a top-level `CerebrumError` hierarchy for the core subsystem mirroring what lexicon already has. Retire bare `Exception(...)` raises in the LLM layer; map them into the appropriate hierarchy. At lexicon/core/engine.py:154, replace `ValueError` with `ConfigurationError` to stay consistent with the declared hierarchy.

findings.summary: InstrumentalCase.execute_operation returns None when the requested operation is not found on the model, instead of raising an error, creating a silent failure mode.
evidence: instrumental.py lines 144-151: `if hasattr(model, operation) and callable(getattr(model, operation)): ... return result` / `else: logging.warning(...); return None` ā€” callers receive None with no indication that the operation was not found.

This contrasts with model.py lines 123, 135 which raise NotImplementedError for unimplemented interface methods ā€” the two failure modes for a missing capability are inconsistent within the same framework.
findings.suggestion: Raise a specific error (e.g., `AttributeError` or a custom `OperationNotFoundError`) instead of returning None. If None-return is required for backward compatibility, at minimum raise in strict mode and document the silent-failure contract explicitly.

findings.summary: src/lexicon/visualization/animated_graph.py swallows at least six distinct Exception catches with only a log.error or log.warning and no propagation, meaning animation generation failures are invisible to callers.
evidence: Lines 50-51: `except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Failed to create graph construction animation: {e}")` ā€” function returns None.
Lines 58-59, 66-67, 179-180, 271-272, 319-320, 342-343: all follow the same catch-and-log pattern with no re-raise.
Callers cannot distinguish a successfully produced animation from a silently failed one without inspecting side-effects (presence of output files).
findings.suggestion: Either re-raise after logging (`raise`) or return a typed result/success indicator that callers can check. Where failure is expected and recoverable, document it explicitly rather than silently returning None.
